title: Sandbag Technique
text:
During an emergency, speed is important, but technique is vital. A poorly built wall will fail when the water pressure rises. Follow these steps to ensure our sanctuary stays dry.



Materials: You will need burlap or plastic sandbags, coarse sand (avoid fine “play sand” if possible), shovels, and heavy-duty gloves.
The Two-Person Team: One person holds the bag while the second person shovels.
Tip: The person holding the bag should stand with their knees slightly bent to protect their back. Fold the top of the bag over to create a “handle” for easier filling.
Don’t Overfill: Fill bags only 1/2 to 2/3 full. This allows the bag to be “squished” into the shape of the bag below it, creating a tight seal.





Never just throw bags into a pile. They must be laid with precision.

Clear the Area: Remove all debris, ice, or large rocks from the area where the wall will sit.
The Staggered Fold: Place the first bag with the “tucked” or tied end pointing upstream (the direction the water is coming from).
The “Brick” Pattern: Lay bags like bricks in a wall. The middle of a bag in the second layer should sit directly over the “seam” where two bags meet in the first layer.
Tamp Down: After laying each bag, step on it or hit it with a shovel to flatten it. This removes air pockets and forces the sand to fill the gaps.





If the water is expected to be more than one foot high, you cannot build a vertical wall; it will topple. You must build a Pyramid.

Rule of Thumb: The base of your wall should be three times wider than the wall is high (e.g., If you need a 1-foot high wall, the base should be 3 feet wide).
Caulking the Seams: As you build upward, continue tamping the bags to ensure the layers “lock” together.





Sandbags are porous—water will eventually seep through the fabric. To create a true barrier, use 6-mil plastic sheeting.

Lay the plastic sheeting along the ground and up the wall/door you are protecting before placing the bags.
Place the sandbags on top of the plastic on the ground.
Lay the rest of the plastic over the outside (water side) of the sandbag wall.
Weigh down the top edge of the plastic with another row of sandbags.





Warning: Floodwater is often “blackwater,” meaning it is contaminated with chemicals or sewage.

Wear Gloves: Always wear heavy-duty gloves when removing bags that have been in contact with floodwater.
Do Not Reuse Sand: If the bags were touched by floodwater, the sand is likely contaminated. Do not use it for playgrounds or sandboxes.
Safe Disposal: Follow local municipal guidelines for disposing of “disaster debris”.

title: From Vision to Action
section: The Creation Care and Sustainable Church Task Force
text:
The Creation Care and Sustainable Church Task Force
Building on the foundation laid by the 2022–2024 Creation Care Task Force, a new team was commissioned in September 2025 to turn recommendations into reality. Our mission is to provide congregations with practical tools and theological grounding needed to lead in a changing world.
Preparation is about more than just technical steps. We invite you to explore these real-world stories from throughout our diocese, highlighting how others are putting resilience into practice, allowing us to better relate to our fellow travelers and their experiences. Additionally, if you have a story to share, please contact us. We want to help spread stories of both successes and failures to all our congregations.
We focus our efforts in three vital areas: Mitigation, Adaptation, and Formation.

Mitigation
Lowering our impact. Mitigation is about slowing the progress of climate change by shrinking our environmental footprint. This includes practical steps to lower energy consumption, conserve water, and move toward zero-waste communities.
    Goal: To be better stewards of the resources we use daily.


Adaptation
Building resilience. While we work to slow climate change, we must also prepare for its effects. Adaptation involves taking proactive measures to reduce our vulnerability to climate-related challenges and ensuring our congregations remain safe, prepared, and resilient.
    Goal: To protect our people and our properties in a shifting climate.


Formation
Re-imagining our relationship with Earth. Meaningful change involves emotion, thought, and action. We seek to engage all three as our community consciously considers our relationship with all of God’s Creation, moving away from a mindset of domination and toward acts of loving and responsible stewardship.
    Goal: To honor the Creator by loving and protecting every aspect of God’s creation.

Resource Links

Zero Waste Church: A North Carolina Episcopal Church with a website filled with potential projects for reducing waste.
Keep Texas Beautiful: An organization dedicated to litter cleanup, with guides for group activities and other resources.

For more information, contact Alan Jackson and Robin Bullington.

title: Flooding & Drainage
text:
Goal: To prevent water from entering church structures, maintain the integrity of our foundations, and ensure our facility remains a dry, safe haven for the community during and after a flood event.



Before implementing physical changes, we must understand the “lay of the land”.

Floodplain Mapping: Identify where our buildings sit relative to the 100-year (1% annual chance) and 500-year (0.2% annual chance) floodplains.
Historical Data: Consult local archives to find the “high-water mark” of the last major local flood.
Entry Point Inspection: Walk the perimeter to identify every possible water entry point, including basement windows, utility conduits, and low-threshold doors.





These are physical modifications to the building and grounds to mitigate damage.



Dry Floodproofing: For essential occupied spaces, we aim to make the building watertight. This involves applying waterproof membranes to exterior walls and installing “Flood Shields” (removable barriers) for doors.
⚠️ Critical Maintenance Note: The Weep-Hole Caveat While sealing the building envelope, extreme caution must be exercised regarding weep-holes in masonry or brick veneer. These small gaps are vital for allowing moisture to escape from the wall cavity to prevent rot and mold.
During a Flood: Weep-holes must be temporarily sealed (using specialized flood-rated plugs or shields) to prevent floodwater from entering the wall assembly. Sheets of plastic held by sandbags, or peelable caulk.
Post-Flood: These plugs must be removed immediately once the water recedes. Permanently plugging weep-holes in a misguided attempt at waterproofing will trap condensation inside your walls, leading to structural decay and severe indoor air quality issues.
Wet Floodproofing: For crawlspaces or unfinished basements, we may choose to allow water in to equalize pressure and prevent foundation collapse. This requires installing Hydrostatic Flood Vents and using flood-resistant materials (concrete, tile).
Backflow Prevention: Install check valves on all sewer and floor drain lines to prevent “blackwater” (sewage) from backing up into the church during heavy rains.





Strategic Elevation: Elevate all A/C compressors, furnaces, and electrical panels. While the goal is the Base Flood Elevation (BFE) + 1 foot, at minimum, systems should be raised above the 10–15 year flood level.
Sump Pump Redundancy: Every basement must have a primary sump pump and a battery-powered backup. In high-risk areas, a third “water-powered” backup pump is recommended.






We must manage how water moves across our property to protect both our buildings and our neighbors downstream.

Precision Grading: The ground must slope away from foundations at a minimum of 1 inch per foot for the first 6–10 feet.
Diversionary Infrastructure: - Channels & Swales: Construct parabolic or trapezoidal channels to redirect water around buildings rather than toward them.

Levees: For river-adjacent properties, consider a low-profile earthen levee, designed with a 3:1 slope for stability and maintenance.

High-Capacity Drainage: Upgrade to 6-inch gutters and ensure downspouts discharge at least 10 feet away from the foundation into stabilized areas.
Permeable Parking: When repaving, use permeable pavers or porous asphalt to allow the ground to absorb runoff, reducing the burden on municipal storm drains.





Hardening is only half the battle; we must be ready to act when a “Watch” or “Warning” is issued.



Sandbagging Strategy: If sandbags are part of our plan, follow a “Staggered Brick” pattern with a plastic liner underneath to create a watertight seal.
Asset Protection: Move high-value items (organs, historical records, sound equipment) to the second floor or the highest point in the sanctuary.
Relocation Evaluation: In cases of extreme and repetitive flooding, the leadership must evaluate the long-term viability of the site and consider the “Managed Retreat” to higher ground.





Debris Clearance: Inspect and clear all storm drains, ditches, and diversionary channels.
Sandbag Audit: Check stored sandbags for rot or UV damage.
Evacuation Drills: Train volunteers on how to assist at-risk congregants or neighbors with limited mobility during a flood evacuation.







FEMA P-348: Protecting Building Utility Systems – The definitive guide for utility hardening.
The Silver Jackets – State-led interagency teams that can provide local flood risk data.
How to Use Sandbags - brief guide on sand-bagging tech.

title: Formation
text:
Goal: To move beyond the “climate silence” in our pews and reclaim our role as stewards of God’s handiwork.



There is a persistent myth that people in our pews aren’t concerned about our changing climate. However, data suggests we are suffering from a “perception gap” rather than a lack of concern.

The Reality: 72% of Americans are concerned about climate change.
The Perception: Most people believe only 52% of their neighbors care.
The Result: To avoid perceived conflict, we stop talking about it. This silence inadvertently feeds the myth that the majority doesn’t care, when the opposite is true. The reality is that “climate dismissives” are a small, albeit vocal, minority.

We strive to encourage loving and practical conversation about the challenges of climate change and environmental degradation, informed by accurate information and abundant civility.




Our clergy are often the most prepared to lead this conversation, yet the most hesitant to start it.

Broad Consensus: Nearly 90% of clergy acknowledge that climate change is real.
Human Agency: 60% believe humans play a significant role, while another 30% believe humans play a lesser but still present role.
The Discussion Gap: Despite these beliefs, only half of clergy have discussed the topic with their congregations, and only 25% have addressed it more than once or twice.

We strive to encourage our clergy to actively help create a space where faith and science meet in service to God’s Creation.




We have curated a library of tools to help your congregation engage with Creation Theology at every level.



Preaching Creation: Homiletic resources for integrating stewardship into the liturgical year.
Visionary Leadership: Templates for leading vestries and boards through “green” transitions.





God’s Gardeners: Sunday School curricula focused on the wonder of nature.
Eco-Justice for Teens: Discussion guides for connecting faith with environmental advocacy.





Book Club Recommendations: View our curated list of books on theology, ecology, and hope—complete with reviews and discussion prompts.
Adult Formation Series: Multi-week curricula exploring the biblical foundations of “Creation Care.”






Not every conversation needs to start in a classroom. Sometimes, the best theology is practiced with our hands in the dirt and our feet on the earth.

Stewardship of the Land: Resources for water-wise gardening, xeriscaping, and planting native species on church grounds and at home.
Plalking (Pick-up + Walking): A spiritual practice of “walking and picking up”—caring for the neighborhood while caring for the soul.
Sacred Excursions: Birding and nature walks designed as “contemplative excursions” to reconnect with the local environment.

title: Agent Profile: Creation Care Technical Consultant
text:
You are the Creation Care Technical Consultant for the Episcopal Diocese of Texas (EDOT). Your goal is to support the “Creation Care and Sustainable Church Task Force” in moving from vision to action.



Theological Foundation: Grounded in “Sacramental Stewardship.” Use the language of “visible signs of inward grace” when discussing physical campus upgrades.
Tone: Professional, supportive, and adaptive. Use a touch of wit, but remain focused on the urgency of the climate mission.
Context: Be mindful that we are in “oil country.” Our mission is not antagonistic but transformative—modeling how a diocese linked to the energy industry can lead the way in Net Zero transitions.





The Diocese is working toward a Net Zero goal of 2035–2040. All advice should align with this timeline and the following three pillars: 1. Adaptation: Hardening properties against heat, wind, and flood. 2. Mitigation: Reducing footprints (LEDs, HVAC, Solar). 3. Formation: Breaking “climate silence” through liturgies and native landscaping.



When generating content or code, adhere to these rules: - Format: Default to Quarto Markdown (.qmd) using Bootstrap 5 styling. - Math/Science: Use LaTeX only for formal formulas (e.g., \(Carbon Footprint = \sum (Energy \times Emission Factor)\)). Use standard text for units like 180°C or 10%. - Linking: Assume all files reside in the same root directory. Use relative links: [Mitigation](mitigation.qmd). - Navigation: Every page must include a standard navigation bar linking back to index.qmd, mitigation.qmd, formation.qmd, and adaptation.qmd. - Formatting: When reformatting pages into markdown, do not change the content, but use what is provided verbatim.




Verification: Always prioritize data found in the “Website” Google Drive folder (ID: 14iYUr7i_xa_YBPlSIv9jt-xK0D43yVij).
Conciseness: Provide clear, scannable responses. Avoid “walls of text.”
Next Steps: Always end a response with a high-value suggestion for the next task (e.g., “Would you like me to draft the Water Audit page next?”).

title: Adaptation
text:
Resilience & Adaptation: Safeguarding Our Mission
Natural disasters can disrupt our ministry, but proactive hardening ensures our doors stay open when the community needs us most. Here is our step-by-step approach to minimizing damage and maximizing safety.
The source and reference for most of this material may be found in the Disaster Mitigation document from the Task Force.

1. Identify Your Primary Risks
The first step in stewardship is understanding where we are most vulnerable. Risk is calculated by looking at the likelihood of an event combined with its potential severity.

Action: Review our Risk Matrices Document to see the initial estimates calculated by the Task Force. These prioritize which threats require our immediate attention.



2. Evaluate Potential Adaptations
Once we know the risks, we perform a cost/benefit analysis. We look for “high-impact, low-cost” wins first, followed by long-term capital investments. A good general source for useful information is the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety.
Select a category below to view specific hardening recommendations:



Disaster Basics
Heat Resilience
Wildfire Mitigation


Cold & Freezing
Flooding & Drainage
Wind & Storm Safety


Hail Protection
Lightning Protection
Power Outage Backup





3. Budget & Resource Allocation
We prioritize adaptations that offer the greatest protection for the most people. This isn’t just an expense; it is an investment in our future.

Funding the Work: Check our Potential Funding Sources page for information on grants, denominational aid, and community partnerships.



4. Implementation & Maintenance
A plan only works if it is put into motion. Our final phase involves:

Executing physical upgrades (e.g., roof reinforcement, sump pump installation).
Updating emergency response protocols.
Annual Review: Disasters change, and so should our plan.

title: Mitigation
text:
Goal: To achieve carbon neutrality in all facilities and operations by 2035, serving as a faithful example of environmental stewardship for our communities.



In July 2022, The Episcopal Church’s General Convention committed the entire church to reaching carbon neutrality by 2030. While the direct impact of a single congregation may seem small, our actions stand as vital examples that can inspire others to join in the work of healing God’s creation.




Before jumping into major projects like solar panels, it is vital to first understand your building’s current performance. Simple, low-cost solutions identified through audits often yield the highest immediate impact.



Energy Audit: Perform a professional energy audit to identify where heat is escaping and where electrical loads can be reduced.
Water Audit: Conduct a full water audit to find hidden leaks and inefficient fixtures.
Waste Audit: Identify opportunities to reduce landfill waste through composting, recycling, and eliminating single-use plastics.






These “easy wins” can be implemented quickly and often pay for themselves within months.

Lighting Retrofits: Replace all CFL or incandescent bulbs with LEDs. This can lower lighting electricity usage by 40%, and the bulbs last twice as long. (Be sure to use Energy Star certified bulbs)
Landscape Maintenance: Replace two-stroke, gasoline, and diesel landscaping maintenance equipment with electric equipment, or engage contractors using electric equipment.
Leak Detection: Test all toilets for leaks every 6 months. A single leaking toilet can cost up to $50 per month in wasted water.
Watering Systems: Adjust irrigation to only spray where needed and plant native trees to reduce the long-term need for watering.
Sustainable Hospitality: Encourage parishioners to contribute ceramic coffee mugs to replace single-use plastic cups.
Efficiency Upgrades:

Replace failed dishwashers with Energy Star models.
Install high-efficiency lavatory faucet aerators (.5 to 1.0 GPM).
Insulate all hot water lines with R-3 or higher insulation.






These projects require more planning but offer significant long-term savings.

Smart Climate Control: Install smart or programmable thermostats to optimize heating and cooling.
Building Envelope: Seal or replace leaky ductwork and add ceiling insulation where needed.
Mechanical Upgrades: Replace aging water heaters with high-efficiency heat pump models.
Fixture Upgrades: Replace standard toilets with low-flow models, upgrade faucets.
Resilient Landscaping: Transition to xeriscaping with native plants that can survive drought without supplemental watering.





These “hard” projects represent deep shifts in how your facility interacts with the environment.

HVAC Modernization: Replace end-of-life heating and cooling systems with the highest-efficiency equipment available.
Renewable Energy: Install a solar array to generate clean power on-site.
Water Stewardship: Install rainwater cisterns or graywater systems for landscape needs.
Clean Transportation: Install EV charging stations to support the transition to electric vehicles for staff and parishioners.





Environmental Footprint Document
Mitigation Project Grid

title: About
text:
The Creation Care and Sustainable Church Task Force of the Episcopal Diocese of Texas (EDOT) is a body of lay leaders commissioned to steward our physical and spiritual resources.
Launched in its current form in September 2025, we provide congregations with the technical tools and theological grounding necessary to meet the environmental challenges of our region.






Our Mission: Sacramental Stewardship



We believe that the Earth is a divine gift. Our care for it is a visible sign of an inward and spiritual grace. In the heart of “energy country,” we model radical transformation by treating carbon reduction and property resilience as essential acts of faith.








Reducing our environmental footprint to reach Net Zero by 2035–2040.



Hardening our facilities against heat and flooding to serve as community “refugia.”



Breaking “climate silence” through theological education and liturgical practice.







Diocesan Audits: Set up a framework taht will allow all congregations to do an environmental audit.
Financial Assistance: Developing strategies for congregations to get affordable upgrades of their utilities, including solar panel installations.


Back to Home
